stonetop.summary: The Whitefang Mountains form the northern border of the World’s End, running from the Huffel Peaks in the northwest, all along the Great Wood and the North Manmarch, to the Dread River and beyond…

text.content:
The Whitefang Mountains form the northern border of the World’s End, running from @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.RxUdCkI2lL6gE5IH]{the Huffel Peaks} in the northwest, all along @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6kt1b8ozEREDCi4k]{the Great Wood} and @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.NERZ6p7FNc1pxNWL]{the North Manmarch}, to @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.lGNIQWIn8CPHuM7E]{the Dread River} and beyond in the east. Beyond them lie nothing but @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.xg8MEuSbbEt1wqg8]{the Frozen Wastes}.

The mountains are tall and jagged, steep and forbidding. Yet they hide almost indescribable beauty: waterfalls, deep pine forests, sweeping meadows. They also hide a surprising number of settlements. @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.VZ3YkXn70E1C7TEO]{Barrier Pass} is closest to @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}, but many more lie beyond.

Always

  • Snow-covered peaks, like mighty teeth
  • Wind: howling, whispering, gusting
  • Monkeys screeching and chittering in the valleys, leaping through the trees
  • Prayer-flags fluttering in the wind
  • The elation of reaching a summit; the despair of realizing it was just a ridge, and you’re only half-way

Spring

  • Slopes and vales bright with rhododendron: purple, red, and orange
  • Trickle and rush of snowmelt

• Storms around Tor’s Fist grow stronger; the colors shift to red and yellow
  • A band of kyakaara slips out of the mountains and is eyeing @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}
  • A southern merchant is intent on finding a mountain village, to trade for ivory and fine wool
  • Mountain apes hold an artifact/scrap of lore in their hoard
  • An apprentice shantyara needs help retrieving her dead master’s sword from the bhoka who killed him

Summer

  • Lush green below; stern, colorless peaks above
  • Birdsong echoing and echoing

Autumn

  • Meadows turned brown and red and gold
  • Mist filling the valleys, clouds gliding by above

Winter

  • Snow cloaking the valleys, choking passes
  • Tears freezing, sweat soaking you under warm clothes

Almost straight north of @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}, this lonely mountain juts above its neighbors. It’s not the tallest peak in the Whitefangs, but it’s tall enough. When summer storms roll up from the south and crash against the mountains, the worst of them swirl around Tor’s Fist. They’ll lash the peak with lightning, and in the aftermath, green and purple lights dance around the crown.

What’s up there? Clearly Tor’s Fist holds some mystery, but its nature is for you to decide. Perhaps the mountain’s peak holds…

  • … the tomb of Stormcatcher, or her son, or her lover; … a cleft, from which the Stone itself was carved; … one of the last thirteen Rime Lords, still in stasis, protected by powerful tulpas; … a potent storm-spirit, corrupted by the Things Below, but bound there by ancient Rime Lord magic; … the domain of a powerful, temperamental Fae; … a shattered portal, torn open and ruined; … a people of great stature, who worship Tor; … the last of the Tempest Lords, grumpy and aloof; … some combination of the above; or … something else entirely.

Chart a Course To reach the summit of Tor’s Fist:

  • You’ll need to wait until late spring or summer to have any chance
  • First you must travel to @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.61j2hjMjANZcniEQ]{the Foothills} or @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.VZ3YkXn70E1C7TEO]{Barrier Pass}
  • From there, you must travel to the base of Tor’s Fist
  • It’ll take at least 5 days (from the Foothills) or 6 days (from Barrier Pass), and a corresponding amount of supplies
  • You need a knowledgeable guide or accurate map
  • You need to bring warm clothes, climbing gear, and fuel for cookfires
  • The terrain is treacherous; you risk injury
  • The way will be grueling; you risk exhausting yourselves/your resources
  • The way is perilous, filled with dangers

From the base of Tor’s Fist, you can attempt to reach the summit

  • It’ll take at least 5 days, lest you get altitude sickness
  • You need to bring a skilled mountaineer
  • The climb is treacherous and grueling; you risk injury, exhaustion, or both
  • You risk drawing the attention of whatever it is that’s up there
  • You must brave the final ascent: a sheer climb in heavy winds

Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

1d12 terrain

RollResult
1–2Glacier/snowfield/snowpack
3Lake/river/stream/waterfall
4Waterlogged soil, frozen or muddy
5Forest/woods/stand of trees/shrubs
6Sheer rock face/bluff/escarpment
7Summit/peak/false peak
8–9Steep slope/switchback trails/ridgeline
10Ravine/canyon/gorge
11Gentle slope/meadow/valley/pass
12Cave/cavern/grotto

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1d6 discovery

RollResult
1A change of terrain and roll again.
2A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
3An encounter (see below)
4A point of interest
5A natural resource
6A settlement

1d6 encounter

RollResult
1Monkeys, curious and mischievous, or perhaps signs of mountain apes
2A patrol from a fortress-monastery, suspicious of the PCs
3Someone in dire straits
4Villagers/layfolk, traveling or tending herds, wary
5Mammoth herders, stiffly polite
6A tulpa, a spirit of the wild, or a Fae, curious or indifferent

1d6 point of interest

RollResult
1A settlement (see below), overrun/destroyed by kyakaara
2A place corrupted by the Things Below
3A place haunted by the dead or undying
4A site sacred to the spirits of the wild or perhaps a Fae domain
5–6A ruin or artifact of the Rime Lords

1d6 natural resource

RollResult
1Vein of ore/mineral deposits/valuable stone (slate, marble, pigments, etc.)
2Game, dangerous to hunt: cave bears, mammoth, etc.
3–4Game, hard to reach/prone to bolt: monkeys, hares, goats, etc.
5Useful or valuable flora
6Safe place to rest, take shelter

1d12 settlement

RollResult
1–2Camp of mammoth herders, likely empty/abandoned in warmer months
3–6Farm valley (hamlet), page 484
7–8Farm valley (village), page 484
9–10Lone dwelling: hermit’s cave/hunter’s lodge/shepherd’s camp/etc.
11–12Fortress-monastery

The Whitefangs host a surprising number of settlements. Most are small farming valleys, even more isolated than @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}. But there are also a number of fortress-monasteries, like @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.VZ3YkXn70E1C7TEO]{Barrier Pass}.

Something interesting: The mountain folk’s ancestors were disciples of the Rime Lords, and Rime Lord teachings still influence the culture. But time, isolation, and competing philosophies have led to vastly different local traditions. An idea or practice that is lauded in one village might be ridiculed in the next. And in the village beyond that? It might get you killed.

Something useful: Communities occasionally trade with their nearest neighbors, and folk in one settlement are familiar with the ways of the next—at least well enough to avoid offense or violence. But if you seek a guide or informant, find one who’s actually been there, lest you have your ears filled with exaggerated nonsense.

Nepalese- and Tibetan-inspired: Aandha, Araayin, Bisheej, Choden, Dawa, Dhaar, Dorji, Duga, Fayurma, Hopan, Jamya, Jyat, Keshhay, Ksali, Kundari, Kunza, Lhamo, Mitis, Nobu, Nyado, Passan, Pratar, Puyari, Radhaa, Ravar, Sajadr, Sonam, Supar, Tani, Tashi, Tenzi, Tseri, Wanchu, Yonta.

• Who among you have traveled here before? When and why?
  • Who do you know (in @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.HesP5wBhjA0l9Ffk]{Gordin’s Delve}, perhaps) who hails from the Whitefangs?
  • Why do you think storms swirl around Tor’s Fist? What do you think those strange lights are?
  • Did you once live in a farm valley, or a fortress-monastery?
  • What drove you to leave?
  • What behavior in @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop} is strictly taboo where you once lived?
  • What practice is common where you once lived, but utterly baffling to folk in Stonetop?
  • How is (are) the language(s) of the mountain folk different from those of Stonetop?
  • Do they worship ___ up there? If so, how is that worship different than in Stonetop?

The valleys of the Whitefangs can be surprisingly pleasant. Streams flow almost year-round, freezing over but still running throughout winter. Below the tree line, tall skinny conifers grow in abundance. Many of these vales hold steadings.

Steading names Amchatala, Bhatand, Bhungadi, Dhulgad, Dhurankat, Ghumjun, Heledhan, Khutan, Methala, Naungadi, Potyata, Sakokoch, Thulalu, Thundind.

Size hamlet (~25 to 75 souls) or village (~150 to 350 souls) Population -1 to +1 Prosperity -1 to +1

  • Trade with... ...
  • other farm valleys (food, spouses) ...
  • mammoth herders (ivory, meat, dark ice, see page 322) Resources
  • Farming: terraced fields, barley, lentils
  • Livestock: goats, sheep, milk, meat
  • Fine wool
  • Timber
  • Hunting/fishing
  • Trades: weaving, dyeing, bronzesmithing, etc. Defenses -1 for a hamlet, +0 for a village
  • Some spears, slings, bows
  • Some bronze tools/weapons
  • Protection from a nearby fortress-monastery

For each steading, pick or roll what their neighbors say they are. Then pick or roll up to 2 features and up to 3 things that are true of everyone here and/or no one here. Imagine what a functional steading would look like with these traits. “Odd” behaviors make sense to the locals, and likely help the community survive/cohere/adapt. Ask yourself how!

1d12 neighbors say they are...

  • 1-2 … monstrous/immoral/irresponsible 3-4 … treacherous/greedy/lazy 5-6 … arrogant/brave/reckless 7-8 … boorish/primitive/lazy 9-10 … generous/gullible/friendly 11-12 … wise/noble/holy

1d12 feature

RollResult
1Surroundings are tainted by the Howling Curse
2A bhoka dwells nearby
3Homes are made of dark ice; inhabitants are inured to cold
4Time passes slower here than outside
5Gifted spirit-walkers defend the steading in dreams
6A famous adept or false adept dwells here
7Entrance is secret/well-defended
8Fae live within the steading, hiding or in plain sight
9Useful or valuable flora grows in abundance
10Gingkara serve and protect the locals
11Possess a major arcana
12Weather is warm/pleasant year-round

1d12 everyone here...

  • 1 … abides by a caste system 2 … eats some unappetizing food 3 … writes wishes/prayers on rags, flapping in the wind 4 … is accompanied by a clever, loyal pet 5 … is beautiful/strong/skilled in __/etc. 6 … lives communally, with no personal possessions/space/family 7 … reads/studies/debates the teachings of a/the Rime Lord(s) 8 … refers to (certain?) things/people only indirectly, poetically 9 … speaks truth, however blunt/unkind 10 … stops to pray/sing/meditate/ dance at specific times each day 11 … trains as warriors/practices pacifism 12 … wears/bears a particular type of clothing/accessory/decoration

1d12 no one here...

  • 1 … bears weapons (or type of weapon) 2 … consumes meat/alcohol/___ 3 … cuts their hair/grooms/bathes 4 … discusses a particular topic/practice 5 … eats/sleeps/has sex/ages past a certain point 6 … fears deep water (they swim, fish, etc.) 7 … goes into the woods/up the mountain/beyond the vale/etc. 8 … speaks aloud/inside/to strangers/etc. 9 … touches another’s skin/hair/shadow 10 … shows their skin/face/hair/etc. 11 … speaks ill of others/admits hardship 12 … tolerates a particular color/material

At least a dozen fortress-monasteries remain, scattered throughout the Whitefang Mountains. @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.VZ3YkXn70E1C7TEO]{Barrier Pass} is one of the largest and most powerful. Each is dedicated to preserving the teachings of the Rime Lords—or at least, preserving their version of them!

Size hamlet (~25 to 75 souls) Population +0 Prosperity +0

Trade with nearby farm valleys (food, livestock, wool) Resources

  • Tithes from nearby farm valleys
  • Terraced gardens (barley, lentils)
  • Livestock (goats, sheep, milk, meat)
  • Fine wool
  • Parchment, vellum, ink
  • Trades: bronze-smithing, weaving, dyeing, tanning, scribing, etc.
  • Rime Lord artifacts Defenses +2
  • Guardians
  • At least one adept
  • One or more tulpas
  • Weapons/armor of bronze, maybe dark ice
  • Defensible position

A typical fortress-monastery has 25-50 residents, split between monks and guardians, plus a handful of layfolk. Most receive food and material support from a nearby farm valley or two. In turn, guardians patrol the region and monks (ostensibly) guide, teach, and serve as an example for villagers.

Place each fortress-monastery in a Rime Lord ruin, and create the adept who presides over the monks there. Referring to the previous page, roll or pick what their neighbors say they are, a feature for the fortress-monastery, plus up to 3 things that are true of everyone here and/or no one here. Finally, pick or roll a trait:

1d12 trait

RollResult
1–2Oppresses/extorts/ignores nearby villages, rather than serving them
3–4Recently lost its presiding adept; succession unclear
5–6Home to multiple sects, each led by a different adept
7–8Hides/protects one of the last thirteen Rime Lords
9–10Very small (Population -1, only ~10 to 15 souls); roll again with d8
11–12Relatively large (village, up to ~300 residents); roll again with d8

Use the ruin’s theme(s), the adept’s instinct and moves, and the fortress-monastery’s details to inform the philosophy of the local sect(s). Name the sect(s), mixing and matching words from the list below. A fortress-monastery is typically named after its sect or a prominent local feature.

Ape, Avalanche, Black, Breath, Bronze, Clear, Closed, Cloud, Crystal, Dancing, Dark, Day, Deep, East, Empty, Eye, Fire, Fist, Five, Flower, Flowing, Four, Frost, Grey, Hand, Heart, Ice, Implacable, Inner, Lake, Lonely, Mind, Mountain, Night, North, Open, Outer, Pale, Palm, Relentless, River, Silent, Single, Sky, Snow, South, Spirit, Spring, Still, Stone, Storm, @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.KJUzuYlurcjPxHd7]{Stream}, Three, Tranquil, Two, Unmoved, Waking, West, White, Will, Wind, Winter

Commonly available The following are special items in @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}, but commonly available in the settlements of the Whitefang Mountains—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll for Trade & Barter. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Iron, glass, and southern fineries are usually in demand.

goods value

Fine wool garment 0 bolt 1 cartload (immobile) 2* Parchment, a few sheets 1 Ink, vial and quills 1 Empty book, parchment 1

  • *
  • +1 if sold/traded outside the Whitefangs.

livestock value

When PCs Trade & Barter in a settlement, they use the local Prosperity (with disadvantage if it’s a hamlet). On a 6-, they don’t mark XP; no one here can/wants to trade—maybe try the next settlement?

goods value

  • Robe/shawl, fine wool, intricately 1* patterned (warm, beautiful) Ivory a bit of finery 1 a substantial piece 2 a mammoth tusk (immobile) 3 cartload of mammoth tusks 4 Dark ice (magical, see page 322) a small piece (magical) 1 a large chunk (magical) 2 with special characteristics +1 a functional tool or weapon +2 An object of black iron +2
  • *
  • +1 if sold/traded outside the Whitefangs.

Services and favors PCs don’t need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade people—even if they offer something of suitable Value. If the PCs are well-regarded by locals, they might get these boons for free, or without needing to Persuade anyone, or at a reduced price.

service/favor value

A few nights of simple food and shelter 0 A day’s labor 0 A crew of farmers, working for a week 1 Consultation with a monk 1 Audience with an adept 2 Weeks of guided study with a monk 2 A mountain guide, to lead a trip to... ... a nearby farm-valley 0 ... another known, nearby spot 1 ... a dangerous/unknown spot 2 ... a secret/deadly place 3

Hazards

  • Getting lost (maze of mountains, canyons, passes, and valleys)
  • Sheer rock face to climb, and a reason to climb it
  • Steep slopes, unstable footing (narrow paths, wet stone, scree, thin waterlogged soil, mud, crumbling rock)
  • Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes
  • An unexpected obstacle/hazard: go forward and risk it, or backtrack and lose half a day?
  • Mudslides and rockslides

Harsh mountain weather

  • Baking sun, dry air
  • Fog banks/low clouds, blocking vision and soaking everything
  • Wind: drowning out voices, threatening to toss you aside
  • Rain and wind rolling up from below
  • Rain and sleet: pelting you, soaking you, threatening to wash you away
  • Thunder or lightning: making you flinch/jerk/jolt, triggering rockslides
  • Snow: hiding a drop, burying a pass, making trails impassible

Brutal cold, leading to hypothermia & frostbite

  • Avalanche
  • Everyone who lives in these mountains learns to spot the signs.
  • Instinct to gather mass, to bury all in its path
  • Have one cascade down a distant slope, majestic and terrifying
  • Point out the snowpack, looming on the slopes above
  • Note how a storm adds mass to the snowpack
  • Point out the dimples as sun/warmth undermine its structure

When an avalanche starts above them, start the following:

  • A chunk of snow gives way
  • It gains mass, momentum; it’ll be here in a matter of minutes
  • It rushes towards them, imminent
  • They’re buried under snow and ice (d8+2 damage, forceful, ignores armor)
  • They’re trapped, disoriented, suffering hypothermia & frostbite (see below)
  • Hypothermia & frostbite
  • It gets cold in the mountains. Very, very cold.
  • Start them shivering, despite fire/ warm clothing
  • Test their endurance on even simple tasks
  • Have them lose their footing/grip
  • Inflict a debility
  • Have frostbite set in (d6 damage, ignores armor)
  • Have frostbite get worse (+messy)
  • Compel them to do something foolish (go to sleep, shed clothing, etc.)
  • Altitude sickness
  • Dangerously thin air starts a couple thousand feet below the snowline, and gets worse the higher you go. Locals are accustomed to the thin air, but even they must be careful.
  • Test their endurance to keep going, or just to breathe
  • Inflict a debility
  • Reveal that they’ve missed something
  • Have them collapse, unable to continue
  • Fill their lungs with fluid, swell their brains, have them roll for Death’s Door

Something interesting: Disciples of the Rime Lords practice techniques to thrive in the thin air, but they take months or years to master.

Something useful: One can acclimate safely by ascending no more than ~1,000 feet each day, and even then, extreme exertion can be dangerous. A tea steeped from purple drakewort can alleviate symptoms, but an overdose can cause death by suffocation.

Creatures the bestiary catalogues in and around The Whitefang Mountains. Click through for full stat blocks and lore.

Folk you might meet in and around The Whitefang Mountains. Click through for full stat blocks and lore.

  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.uGh38GPx9hdbQ0XH]{Guardian}
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.AXCc64h6QQiyBGpx]{Mammoth Herder}
